platforms.name: NNDSS
platforms.description: National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System for US reportable conditions
weaknesses: Limited to US domestic coverage with no international data integration

profile.overview: The National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System (NNDSS) is the CDC's foundational case surveillance system collecting reportable disease data from all US states and territories. Operational since 1961 (predecessor system since 1878).
profile.functional_scope: Collects case-level surveillance data for 120+ notifiable conditions (infectious and non-infectious) from state/territorial health departments. Produces MMWR weekly reports, annual summary tables, and provisional data. Supports Message Mapping Guide (MMG) standardized reporting.
profile.tech_stack: HL7 messaging (transitioning to FHIR). National Electronic Disease Surveillance System (NEDSS) as backend. BioSense Platform integration. CDC WONDER for public data access. EDX (Enterprise Data Exchange) for case notifications.
profile.operator: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), Atlanta, GA, USA. Division of Health Informatics and Surveillance. All 50 states, DC, and territories report mandatorily.
profile.data_model: Case-level surveillance data with standardized fields per condition (demographics, lab results, outcomes). MMG format for electronic transmission. Deidentified aggregates published weekly/annually. Provisional vs finalized data distinction.
profile.users_scale: Covers the entire US population (~330M). Every state/territorial health department reports. Data used by thousands of epidemiologists, researchers, and policymakers. MMWR reaches millions of public health professionals.
profile.access_model: Public aggregate data via CDC WONDER and MMWR tables. Case-level data restricted to authorized public health personnel. No registration for published tables. Research access through CDC data request process.

deep_research.executive_summary: NNDSS (National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System) is CDC's foundational national case-based reporting system for 120+ notifiable conditions in the US. Score 83.8/100. Statutory backbone of US disease surveillance. Transitioning from HL7 to FHIR-based electronic case reporting. Aggregated data public; case-level restricted. Weakness: reporting latency and data quality variability across jurisdictions.

platforms.name: ReportStream
platforms.description: CDC's free cloud-based data exchange pipeline for routing public health test results from labs to health departments using HL7/FHIR standards

profile.overview: ReportStream is CDC's free, interoperable data platform that connects healthcare organizations, labs, and public health agencies for streamlined electronic reporting of public health data. It is a key component of CDC's Data Modernization Initiative.
profile.functional_scope: Automated routing and transformation of public health reports from healthcare providers and labs to state and local health departments. Supports Electronic Lab Reports (ELR), Electronic Case Reports (eCR), and Electronic Test Orders and Results (ETOR). Data format translation between HL7v2, FHIR, and CSV. Universal data pipeline with configurable routing rules.
profile.tech_stack: Kotlin/Java backend, React.js frontend, Azure cloud infrastructure. Supports HL7v2.5.1, FHIR R4, CSV data formats. REST API with OAuth 2.0 authentication. Open-source on GitHub (USDS/prime-reportstream). PostgreSQL database.
profile.operator: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), built by the U.S. Digital Service (USDS) and contractors.
profile.data_model: HL7-structured public health messages with patient, specimen, test, result, and provider data. FHIR Bundle resources for modern interoperability. Configurable schema mapping per jurisdiction.
profile.users_scale: Connected to thousands of labs and healthcare facilities. Routes millions of reports monthly to 60+ state and territorial health departments across the United States.
profile.access_model: Free for U.S. public health entities. API-based onboarding. Open-source codebase.

deep_research.executive_summary: ReportStream is CDC's free, open-source cloud-based data exchange pipeline for routing electronic laboratory reports (ELR), electronic case reports (eCR), and electronic test orders/results (ETOR) to public health authorities. platforms.name: WastewaterSCAN
platforms.description: Stanford-led national wastewater surveillance tracking 12+ infectious disease targets

profile.overview: WastewaterSCAN is a Stanford University-led national wastewater surveillance network launched in 2020 by Professors Alexandria Boehm and Marlene Wolfe. It monitors infectious disease trends through wastewater analysis across the United States.
profile.functional_scope: Tracks 12+ infectious disease targets in wastewater including SARS-CoV-2, RSV, Influenza A/B, Norovirus, Mpox, and emerging pathogens. profile.overview: CBRNResponder is FEMA's National Common Operating Platform concept: a single, secure platform for CBRN incident data-sharing and multi-hazard event management. Integrates ChemResponder, RadResponder and BioResponder (in development), plus the IMAAC Portal with 24/7 support.
profile.functional_scope: Supports multi-hazard event management and CBRN data sharing, with 24/7 support hotline and monthly training webinars. Enables common event space across organisations. Governance+workflow platform integrating federal assets and policy guidance.
profile.tech_stack: Tech stack unconfirmed publicly. Web-based with identity/login portals. Data standards (NIEM, CAP) unconfirmed. Deployment is cloud/SaaS under FEMA governance.
profile.operator: Operated by FEMA (USA). USFA blog describes 24/7 hotline and ongoing training. Multi-agency integration with DHS S&T, EPA and DOE inputs.
profile.data_model: Event-centric common event space linking chemical/radiological incidents and plume modelling outputs (via IMAAC) plus responder data. Exact schemas unconfirmed.
profile.users_scale: USFA blog reports ~2,200 organisations and 13,000 responders. FEMA tools page variant reports 1,500+ organisations in common event space.
profile.access_model: Free-to-use FEMA platform (US model) with registration/login; not open source. For other nations, treat as a design pattern rather than directly adoptable software.

deep_research.executive_summary: FEMA CBRNResponder Network is the US national common operating platform concept for CBRN incident data-sharing, integrating RadResponder, ChemResponder, BioResponder (in development), and IMAAC Portal. Score 85/100. Thousands of organisations engaged. 24/7 hotline and monthly training. Governance and workflow integration across federal/state/local responders.

platforms.name: ARGOS DSS
platforms.description: Mature multi-hazard decision support system for CBRN emergency management, operational in 13 countries covering 400M+ people
weaknesses: Not a full incident platform alone

profile.overview: ARGOS is a mature decision support system for emergency management of CBRN releases. Supports organisations in 13 countries covering 400+ million people. ARGOS User Group founded in 2001. Covers nuclear/radiological and chemical scenarios with 2500+ chemical specifications.
profile.functional_scope: GIS-based consequence assessment: integrates monitoring data and atmospheric dispersion models. Nuclear/radiological and chemical modules. Chemical modules include URD (urban dispersion) and heavy gas models covering 2500+ chemicals. External long-range models can be started remotely at national Met Offices.
profile.tech_stack: Client runs on Microsoft Windows; can run standalone or coupled to central MS SQL Server. External models loosely coupled via ARGOS. Languages/APIs unconfirmed.
profile.operator: Developed by PDC and Danish Emergency Management Agency (DEMA). Consortium-driven with user involvement. Ireland's NEPNA has used ARGOS since 2007.
profile.data_model: Ingests measurement data from airborne/mobile/stationary stations plus incident source terms, meteorology and GIS. Outputs forecasts, doses, contamination maps and decision-support artefacts.
profile.users_scale: 13 countries: Australia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Brazil, Canada, Denmark, Estonia, Ireland, Lithuania, Montenegro, Norway, Poland, Sweden, New Zealand. 400M+ people coverage.
profile.access_model: Commercial/consortium model. Licensing terms, APIs and redistribution rights unconfirmed.

deep_research.executive_summary: ARGOS is a mature multi-hazard decision support system for emergency management of CBRN releases, used in 13 countries. Score 86/100. Developed by PDC (Prolog Development Centre) and Danish Emergency Management Agency (DEMA). GIS-based consequence assessment with atmospheric dispersion models. Supports nuclear, radiological, chemical, and biological scenarios.

platforms.name: RODOS / JRODOS
platforms.description: Radiological emergency prognosis and decision support system, Java-based JRODOS operational since 2010, used in Germany/Switzerland

profile.overview: RODOS (Realtime Online Decision Support System) is a radiological emergency prognosis and decision support model. Germany's BfS uses RODOS as part of IMIS. Java-based JRODOS became operational since 2010. Swiss ENSI confirms JRODOS use for atmospheric dispersion calculations.
profile.functional_scope: Computes future contamination and expected doses in radiological emergencies and underpins protective measures recommendations. Multi-pathway models applicable across emergency phases. Radiological/nuclear focused (not chemical).
profile.tech_stack: Java-based JRODOS operational since 2010. Evolution from UNIX to Java. DB, GIS libraries not detailed in government pages. Deployed at government authority sites.
profile.operator: Germany: BfS Federal Radiological Situation Centre uses RODOS alongside ELAN. ELAN operational since 2001 supporting ~70 institutions. KIT (Karlsruhe Institute of Technology) maintains JRODOS.
profile.data_model: Inputs: meteorological data, monitoring network data, radionuclide release scenarios. Outputs: dose forecasts, contamination maps and protective action recommendations.
profile.users_scale: Germany: federal-level use in BfS/IMIS. ELAN: ~70 institutions. International: ENSI (Switzerland). Broader country counts unconfirmed.
profile.access_model: Government/authority deployments; licensing restricted. Not open source. Access limited to competent authorities and partners.

deep_research.executive_summary: RODOS (Real-time Online Decision Support) / JRODOS (Java-based RODOS) is a radiological emergency prognosis and decision support system. Score 85/100. JRODOS operational since 2010. Used in Germany and Switzerland. Multi-pathway dose assessment models. Predicts contamination, doses, and supports protective action decisions. Developed by KIT (Germany).

Ongoing KIT development for enhanced modelling capabilities.

platforms.name: CAMEO Suite (EPA/NOAA)
platforms.description: Free EPA/NOAA software suite for chemical emergency planning and response (ALOHA dispersion model, CAMEO Chemicals, MARPLOT mapping)

profile.overview: CAMEO (Computer-Aided Management of Emergency Operations) is a US EPA/NOAA software suite for chemical emergency planning and response. Updated Feb 2026. Widely distributed as free software. Includes 4 core programs.
profile.functional_scope: CAMEO Data Manager (planning data), CAMEO Chemicals (chemical datasheets + reactivity prediction), MARPLOT mapping, and ALOHA dispersion model for threat zones of chemical releases. Supports fires/explosions scenarios.
profile.tech_stack: Desktop software suite (not cloud platform). GIS integration via MARPLOT. Data standards (HL7/FHIR) not applicable. Integration into backbone typically via exports and procedural use.
profile.operator: Developed/maintained by US EPA and NOAA. Free distribution. Public sector funded.
profile.data_model: Stores community chemical planning data (facilities, inventories, contacts, routes, incidents, resources) and chemical properties/datasheets. ALOHA outputs threat zones. MARPLOT displays GIS layers.
profile.users_scale: Used widely per official directories. Specific user counts, MAU and deployment numbers unconfirmed. Active maintenance (CAMEO Chemicals v3.0.0).
profile.access_model: Free software suite distributed by EPA/NOAA; downloadable publicly. Not a secure national CBRN operational backbone; best as chemical planning tool integrated into procedures.

deep_research.executive_summary: CAMEO (Computer-Aided Management of Emergency Operations) Suite is EPA/NOAA's set of free software tools for chemical emergency planning and response. Score 80/100. Includes CAMEO Data Manager, CAMEO Chemicals, MARPLOT (mapping), and ALOHA (atmospheric dispersion). Updated Feb 2026. Used by thousands of US first responders and LEPCs. True CBRN Operational Platform (chemical domain).

platforms.name: HPAC
platforms.description: US DoD/DTRA CBRN dispersion modelling system, primary model for IMAAC emergency response plumes

profile.overview: HPAC is a CBRN dispersion modelling system associated with US DoD/DTRA. EPA states HPAC is the primary model used by DTRA for IMAAC emergency response plumes. Supports releases of chemical/biological/nuclear material into atmosphere/enclosed spaces.
profile.functional_scope: Predicts effects of contamination released into atmosphere; uses meteorological and geographic information to map contaminated areas and support resource estimation. Modelling engine, not a full incident platform.
profile.tech_stack: ORNL report describes distributed object architecture: CORBA, EJB, SOAP/WSDL, Java/J2EE/J2SE, XML and GIS artefacts. Windows PC requirement. ~20-25 GB free disk for archived met data. SCIPUFF transport/diffusion code.
profile.operator: Developed by DSWA/DTRA (US DoD). Used for IMAAC products. NATO/JCBRN COE training engagement. DTRA school HPAC-N course (5-day).
profile.data_model: Inputs: source terms, meteorology, terrain/geography. Outputs: plume maps, concentration/dose fields and graphs. Uses SCIPUFF transport/diffusion code.
profile.users_scale: User counts unconfirmed. DTRA's primary model for IMAAC plumes. Used in NATO modelling/simulation contexts.
profile.access_model: Likely restricted to government/partner users. Not open source. Used operationally via IMAAC requests and within defence communities.

deep_research.executive_summary: HPAC (Hazard Prediction and Assessment Capability) is the US DoD/DTRA's premier CBRN dispersion modelling system. Score 84/100. Used for IMAAC plume modelling products. Supports chemical, biological, nuclear, and radiological release scenarios. Meteorology and geography-based dispersion prediction. Restricted military/government access only.

platforms.name: EURDEP / ECURIE
platforms.category: European Radiological Data Exchange & Early Notification
platforms.description: EU radiological monitoring data exchange platform (EURDEP, est. 1995) and urgent nuclear notification system (ECURIE, 24/7)

profile.overview: EURDEP is the European Radiological Data Exchange Platform for near-real-time exchange of national radiological monitoring network data. Established 1995. ECURIE provides 24/7 early notification of radiological accidents/emergencies, operated by the European Commission. JRC notes EURDEP is not a rapid alert system.
profile.functional_scope: EURDEP enables continuous exchange of monitoring data among participating countries. ECURIE delivers early notification of radiological accidents. Data should be provided at least daily normally and at least hourly during an emergency. 39 countries linked as of 2024.
profile.tech_stack: Technical implementation details, protocols and schemas not fully specified. Web-ECURIE referenced. Security mechanisms and APIs unconfirmed. European Commission operated/hosted.
profile.operator: European Commission services (JRC provides EURDEP portal; EC operates ECURIE 24/7). Legal basis: Euratom Council Decision 87/600 and BSS Directive 2013/59.
profile.data_model: EURDEP exchanges radiological monitoring network data near-real-time. ECURIE exchanges urgent notifications and info messages. Participating countries: 39 (2024).
profile.users_scale: 39 countries linked in EURDEP as of 2024. User/organisation account counts not public.
profile.access_model: Institutional access for member/participant authorities. Some public monitoring data available via EURDEP site. ECURIE restricted to competent authorities.

deep_research.executive_summary: EURDEP (European Radiological Data Exchange Platform) provides near-real-time radiological data exchange across 39 countries; ECURIE (European Community Urgent Radiological Information Exchange) provides 24/7 emergency notification. Score 83/100. Operated by European Commission/JRC. Critical international radiological data sharing infrastructure. Hourly updates during emergencies.

platforms.name: IAEA USIE
platforms.description: IAEA's secure website for Member States to exchange urgent information during nuclear/radiological incidents with encryption and 2FA

profile.overview: USIE is IAEA's secure website for Member State and Convention contact points to exchange urgent information during nuclear/radiological incidents and for INES reporting. 1,000+ users from 150+ Member States. Launched during Fukushima response (2011).
profile.functional_scope: Supports emergency notifications, follow-up info, requests/offers of assistance, and INES reporting. International notification channel that national backbones must interoperate with. Not a national COP/incident platform.
profile.tech_stack: Role-based access, two-factor authentication, and encryption of exchanged/stored information. IRIX international data standard implemented for structured exchange. Underlying software stack unconfirmed.
profile.operator: Operated by IAEA Incident and Emergency Centre (IEC). Developed since 2009. IAEA/member-supported funding.
profile.data_model: Structured event information including installation status, releases and measured radiation levels via IRIX, plus short messages within events. Users designated by Member States with role-based access.
profile.users_scale: 1,000+ users from 150+ Member States registered. Earlier citation: 1,200+ users from 150+ countries.
profile.access_model: Restricted to designated officials/contact points; not public. Not open source. IAEA account provisioning with 2FA. Public event summaries on NEWS site.

platforms.name: CTBTO IDC
platforms.category: Global Nuclear Test Monitoring Data Products
platforms.description: CTBTO International Data Centre processing IMS data into automated event lists (SEL) and Reviewed Event Bulletins (REB) for treaty verification

profile.overview: CTBTO's International Data Centre processes IMS data and distributes products to States Signatories. SEL3 produced within 6 hours (automatic), then analyst-reviewed REB within two days. Treaty monitoring via seismic, hydroacoustic, infrasound and radionuclide data.
profile.functional_scope: Produces automated Standard Event Lists (SEL1/SEL2/SEL3) and analyst-reviewed products (REB). Upstream nuclear-event detection dependency for national monitoring and verification. Not a national first-responder platform.
profile.tech_stack: Digital signature embedded in station data flow and tamper-detection devices on equipment enclosures. Internal software stack not disclosed. Automated then analyst-reviewed processing.
profile.operator: Operated by CTBTO Preparatory Commission/Provisional Technical Secretariat in Vienna. Supports National Data Centres (NDCs4All initiative). Funded by States Signatories.
profile.data_model: IMS waveform + radionuclide data; event lists (SEL1/2/3) and bulletins (REB). IMS comprises 321 monitoring stations + 16 labs when complete. SEL3 timeline: 6 hours; REB: ~2 days.
profile.users_scale: States Signatories and National Data Centres. IMS facilities hosted by 89 countries. ~90% of planned facilities operating.
profile.access_model: Restricted to States Signatories/authorised NDCs. Data/products distributed under CTBT verification arrangements.

deep_research.executive_summary: CTBTO International Data Centre processes data from the International Monitoring System (IMS) into automated event lists (SEL) and analyst-reviewed Reviewed Event Bulletins (REB) for nuclear test treaty verification. Score 83/100. 337 monitoring facilities worldwide (seismic, hydroacoustic, infrasound, radionuclide). Operated by CTBTO Preparatory Commission in Vienna. Treaty verification foundation with dual civilian applications.

2024: Continued IMS buildout; 337 of planned facilities operational.

platforms.name: SCADACore EnviroLive CBRNE
platforms.description: Web-based remote monitoring platform for HSE & CBRNE sensors with cloud dashboards and data archival

profile.overview: SCADACore markets IIoT Live / EnviroLive as web-based remote monitoring for HSE & CBRNE sensors, providing cloud dashboards and data archival. Positioned as vendor-agnostic monitoring system for distributed sensor networks.
profile.functional_scope: Collects data from any CBRNE field device, brings data online, enables real-time viewing/analysis with reports and warnings. Core function: monitoring + alerting. Plume modelling and doctrine reporting not evidenced.
profile.tech_stack: Technical stack and standards unconfirmed. Security: secure, redundant servers and secure remote login. GIS standards and APIs not specified publicly.
profile.operator: Operated commercially by SCADACore. Funding and procurement model: commercial subscriptions.

profile.users_scale: User counts/countries and CBRNE-specific deployments unconfirmed. Claims applicability to companies and government agencies.
profile.access_model: Commercial hosted service (cloud) with web login. On-prem options not evidenced. API access and terms unconfirmed.

platforms.name: RadResponder
platforms.description: FEMA-supported free platform for standardised collection and management of radiological data across US responders
strengths: US National Standard for rad data

profile.overview: RadResponder is a FEMA-supported, free web-based platform for standardised collection/management of radiological data across US responders. DHS PIA-054 (Aug 2019; updated Apr 2025) documents privacy coverage.
profile.functional_scope: Standardises how organisations collect/store/use/manage radiological data to analyse site dangers and support mitigation decisions. Positioned as National Standard for radiological data management.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based; mobile and field data collection central. DHS PIA indicates PII handling/protection measures. APIs and standards unconfirmed.
profile.operator: Operated by FEMA (USA) with DHS S&T, EPA and DOE support. Multi-agency development inputs.
profile.data_model: Radiological measurements, site/sample records, responder/team tracking and associated metadata. DHS PIA confirms PII is collected and protected.
profile.users_scale: Part of CBRNResponder ecosystem (1,500+ orgs). RadResponder-specific counts not separated publicly.
profile.access_model: Free to eligible response organisations; registration required. Not open source. Privacy documented in DHS/FEMA PIA-054.

deep_research.executive_summary: RadResponder is a FEMA-supported free platform for standardised radiological data collection and management across US responders. Score 82/100. Standardises radiological data practices to analyse site dangers and support mitigation decisions. Part of the FEMA CBRNResponder Network. Free for US government and response organisations.

FEMA Radiological Data Management Tools

profile.overview: IMAAC is a US federally supported capability providing no-cost expert support for incidents involving hazmat/CBRNE agents. FEMA states IMAAC coordinates, develops and disseminates plume models representing the federal position during atmospheric release emergencies.
profile.functional_scope: Provides coordinated federal dispersion modelling/hazard prediction products. Service/portal for plume products, not a full incident management platform. Interfaces with CBRNResponder as a portal component.
profile.tech_stack: HPAC is the primary model used by DTRA for IMAAC plumes. HPAC runs on Windows PC, ~20-25 GB free disk for archived met data. Portal implementation details unconfirmed.
profile.operator: US federal interagency framework; FEMA is public-facing operator. DHS S&T funded development of tools available through IMAAC.
profile.data_model: Consumes incident source terms, meteorology, geography to generate plume maps and related products. Outputs disseminated to SLTT responders.
profile.users_scale: Supports federal and SLTT first responders, emergency managers, public/environmental health practitioners. Usage metrics unconfirmed.
profile.access_model: No-cost expert support per FEMA; access via FEMA/IMAAC activation processes and portal. Not open source.
